---
title: "09 · Square Roots and Irrationals"
subtitle: "Undoing squaring — and meeting numbers that aren't fractions"
lesson-id: "t2.square-roots-irrationals"
tier: 2
prerequisites: ["t2.exponents-powers"]
unlocks: ["t2.pythagorean-theorem"]
applied-domains: ["research", "encryption"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [07 · Exponents](../07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute $7^2 = 49$ and $12^2 = 144$?
3. Comfortable with the idea of an *inverse operation* (subtraction
   undoes addition, division undoes multiplication)?
:::

## Touch

Pick a number $a$. The widget shows $\sqrt{a}$ — and tells you whether
the square root is **rational** (a fraction) or **irrational** (not).

```{ojs}
viewof aVal = Inputs.range([0, 100], {value: 16, step: 1, label: "a:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const sq = Math.sqrt(aVal);
  const isInt = Number.isInteger(sq);
  const knownIrrational = !isInt && aVal > 0;
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1.6rem;">
      √<span style="color:#c2185b; font-weight:bold;">${aVal}</span>
      &nbsp;=&nbsp;
      <span style="color:#1f2933; font-weight:bold;">
        ${isInt ? sq : sq.toFixed(6) + '...'}
      </span>
    </div>
    <div style="font-size: 1rem; margin-top:0.4rem; color:${isInt ? '#2e7d32' : '#c2185b'};">
      ${aVal === 0 ? '√0 = 0 (boundary case)'
        : isInt ? `Rational! (${sq})² = ${aVal} exactly`
        : `Irrational — decimal continues forever, no repeating pattern`}
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

The square root of a *perfect square* (1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ...) is a whole
number. Otherwise it is **irrational** — its decimal goes on forever
without ever repeating.

## Wonder

1. What is $\sqrt{16}$? $\sqrt{25}$? $\sqrt{100}$?
2. What is $\sqrt{2}$? Roughly?
3. Could $\sqrt{2}$ be written as a fraction $\frac{p}{q}$?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Compute (without a calculator): $\sqrt{1}, \sqrt{4}, \sqrt{9}, \sqrt{36}, \sqrt{81}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1, 2, 3, 6, 9$. The "perfect squares" are the squares of the natural
numbers; their square roots are whole.
</details>

### Problem 2

Estimate $\sqrt{50}$ without a calculator.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$50$ is between $49 = 7^2$ and $64 = 8^2$. So $\sqrt{50}$ is between
$7$ and $8$. Closer to which?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{50}$ is just slightly above 7 — closer to 7 than to 8. Try
$7.07$: $7.07^2 \approx 49.98$. So $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$.

Estimating by *bracketing between perfect squares* is a useful
technique that survives all the way into high-end engineering — quick
sanity checks on calculator output.
</details>

### Problem 3

Use exponent rules to simplify $\sqrt{a^2}$ for $a \ge 0$. (Recall
$\sqrt{a} = a^{1/2}$ — the square root is the half-power.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sqrt{a^2} = (a^2)^{1/2} = a^{2 \cdot 1/2} = a^1 = a$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{a^2} = a$ (for $a \ge 0$). And $\sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{a} = a$ —
that's the *definition*.

Square root and squaring are **inverse operations**, just like
addition/subtraction and multiplication/division. The notation
$a^{1/2}$ for $\sqrt{a}$ extends the exponent laws to fractional
exponents — we just discovered fractional exponents implicitly.
</details>

### Problem 4

Compute $\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{8}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{b} = \sqrt{ab}$ (since both sides squared equal
$ab$).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{8} = \sqrt{16} = 4$.

The product of two irrationals can be rational. Often, simplifying a
product of square roots reveals a clean answer.
</details>

### Problem 5

Suppose $\sqrt{2} = \dfrac{p}{q}$ for some integers $p, q$ in lowest
terms (no common factor). Squaring: $2 = \dfrac{p^2}{q^2}$, so $p^2 =
2 q^2$.

This means $p^2$ is *even*, so $p$ must be even. Write $p = 2k$:
$(2k)^2 = 2q^2$, i.e. $4k^2 = 2q^2$, i.e. $2k^2 = q^2$. So $q^2$ is
also even, so $q$ is even.

But then $p$ and $q$ are *both even* — contradicting "lowest terms".

What does this prove?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$\sqrt{2}$ cannot be written as a fraction** — it is **irrational**.

This is the famous proof, attributed to the school of Pythagoras
(c. 500 BCE). Legend has it that the Pythagoreans had built their
philosophy on the belief that *all numbers are rational*, and
the discovery of irrationals was so disturbing that they tried to keep
it secret — and supposedly drowned the discoverer (Hippasus) at sea.
The truth of the legend is debated; the truth of the proof is not.

Since then we know that *most* numbers are irrational. Beyond
$\sqrt{2}$: $\sqrt{3}, \sqrt{5}, \sqrt{6}, \ldots$ are all irrational
(unless the input is a perfect square). $\pi$ is irrational. $e$ (the
calculus constant) is irrational.

The number system extends from naturals → integers → rationals →
**reals**. The reals contain both rational and irrational numbers.
We will build the reals carefully in Tier 5.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The Babylonians were computing $\sqrt{2}$ to astonishing accuracy nearly
**four thousand years ago**. A clay tablet known as **YBC 7289**
(c. 1800–1600 BCE), now at Yale, shows $\sqrt{2}$ written in
sexagesimal (base 60) as $1; 24, 51, 10$ — equal to
$1.41421296\ldots$, correct to *six decimal places*. They found it
without calculators, without algebra, and roughly 1300 years before
the Pythagoreans proved it irrational.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A square root is a length the eye can see but the digits can never
finish writing. The diagonal of a unit square *exists* — you can draw
it — yet no fraction names it. Geometry hands us numbers that
arithmetic alone could never have invented.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **square root** $\sqrt{a}$ of a non-negative number $a$ is the
non-negative number whose square is $a$:
$$\sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{a} = a.$$
Equivalently, $\sqrt{a} = a^{1/2}$ — square root is the half-power.

For $a < 0$, $\sqrt{a}$ is not a real number (we will meet *complex
numbers* in Tier 6).

A real number is **rational** if it can be written as a fraction
$\dfrac{p}{q}$ of integers ($q \neq 0$); otherwise it is **irrational**.

**Key facts:**

- $\sqrt{ab} = \sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{b}$.
- $\sqrt{a/b} = \sqrt{a} / \sqrt{b}$.
- $\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, \sqrt{5}, \pi, e, \ldots$ are all irrational.
- The set of rationals + irrationals = the **real numbers**, $\mathbb{R}$.

:::

> *Square root undoes squaring — the inverse operation;*
> *Half-power notation: $\sqrt{a} = a^{1/2}$;*
> *Most numbers are irrational — including $\sqrt{2}$, proved by Pythagoras.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Simplify $\sqrt{72}$.

A competent solver factors out *perfect squares*:

1. $72 = 36 \times 2 = 6^2 \times 2$.
2. $\sqrt{72} = \sqrt{36 \cdot 2} = \sqrt{36} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 6\sqrt{2}$.

**Answer: $6\sqrt{2}$.** This is the *simplified radical form* — pull
out perfect-square factors. The remaining $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational and
can't be reduced further.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute: $\sqrt{49}, \sqrt{121}, \sqrt{0.25}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7, 11, 0.5$.
</details>

**W2.** Estimate: $\sqrt{30}$, $\sqrt{200}$ (give whole-number bounds).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{30}$ is between 5 ($5^2=25$) and 6 ($6^2 = 36$); closer to 5.
$\sqrt{200}$ is between 14 ($14^2 = 196$) and 15 ($15^2 = 225$);
closer to 14.
</details>

**W3.** Simplify: $\sqrt{20}$, $\sqrt{50}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{20} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 5} = 2\sqrt{5}$.
$\sqrt{50} = \sqrt{25 \cdot 2} = 5\sqrt{2}$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Simplify $\sqrt{75} + \sqrt{12}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Simplify each first; then combine like radicals.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{75} = 5\sqrt{3}$. $\sqrt{12} = 2\sqrt{3}$. Sum: $7\sqrt{3}$.
Like radicals (same number under the root) add like algebraic terms.
</details>

**P2.** Solve $x^2 = 25$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
*Two* numbers square to 25.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = 5$ **or** $x = -5$. Both square to 25. The *positive* root is
$\sqrt{25} = 5$ by convention; but the *equation* $x^2 = 25$ has two
solutions, written $x = \pm \sqrt{25} = \pm 5$.
</details>

**P3.** Solve $x^2 = 18$. Give the answer in simplified radical form.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = \pm \sqrt{18} = \pm \sqrt{9 \cdot 2} = \pm 3\sqrt{2}$.

The solutions are *irrational* — exact values that no decimal fully
captures. Solving quadratic equations (Lesson 14) often produces such
irrational solutions.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A right triangle has both shorter sides of length 1. What is
the length of its hypotenuse? (Hint: Pythagorean theorem, which we
prove formally next lesson, says hypotenuse² = leg₁² + leg₂².)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$h^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2$, so $h = \sqrt{2}$.

The hypotenuse of a unit right-isoceles triangle is **$\sqrt{2}$**.
This is the original geometric appearance of the irrational number
$\sqrt{2}$ — and is what made the Pythagoreans realise irrationals
exist (Discover P5). Geometry built the question; algebra answered it.
</details>

**S2.** Many encryption schemes (notably **Diffie–Hellman key
exchange**) work *modulo a prime $p$* but use exponents that themselves
have square-root operations on the side. Why might irrational numbers
be inconvenient for cryptography?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Computers store finite digits. Irrationals have infinite, non-
repeating decimals.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Cryptography needs *exact* arithmetic** — the slightest rounding
error can break a key or a signature. Irrationals can only be stored
*approximately*. So cryptography uses **integers and modular
arithmetic** exclusively, where everything is exact.

Yet square roots *do* appear in crypto, in the form of "modular square
roots" — solutions of $x^2 \equiv a \pmod p$. These are integer
solutions, computed exactly, and are central to schemes like
Rabin's cryptosystem.

The takeaway: applied maths often *avoids* irrationals not for
philosophical reasons but for engineering ones (exact storage). This
shapes how cryptography is built. (Tier 7 elaborates.)
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\sqrt{a + b} = \sqrt{a} + \sqrt{b}$."** **False.** $\sqrt{9 +
   16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$, but $\sqrt{9} + \sqrt{16} = 3 + 4 = 7$.
   Square root does *not* distribute over addition.
2. **"Every decimal is rational."** No. Decimals that *terminate* or
   *eventually repeat* are rational. Decimals that go on forever
   *without* repeating (like $\pi$ or $\sqrt{2}$) are irrational.
3. **"$\sqrt{x^2} = x$."** Only if $x \ge 0$. For $x < 0$, $\sqrt{x^2}
   = |x|$. (E.g. $\sqrt{(-3)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3$, not $-3$.)
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [square root](/glossary.qmd#square-root) undoes squaring.** It asks
   "what number, multiplied by itself, gives this?"
2. **Pull perfect-square factors out from under the radical.** That is
   *the* simplification move — $\sqrt{72} = 6\sqrt{2}$, never left as
   $\sqrt{72}$.
3. **Most numbers cannot be written as fractions.** The
   [irrationals](/glossary.qmd#irrational-number) — $\sqrt{2}$, $\pi$, $e$ —
   live on the number line right next to the rationals, and there are
   far more of them.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you walk through the proof that $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational, in
   your own words?
2. Without scrolling back, can you simplify $\sqrt{48}$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $\sqrt{48} = \sqrt{16 \cdot 3} = 4\sqrt{3}$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[10 · The Pythagorean Theorem](../10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (hypotenuse of unit right triangle).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P5* (the irrationality proof).

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[07 · Exponents and Powers](../07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)** —
  $\sqrt{a} = a^{1/2}$ extends the exponent laws to fractional
  exponents.

### What this unlocks

- **[10 · The Pythagorean Theorem](../10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd)** —
  needs square roots to extract side lengths from squared lengths.
- **Tier 5 — Real Analysis** — careful construction of the real
  numbers, including the proof that $\sqrt{2}$ exists as a real
  number (not obvious!).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Irrationals are the bulk of the real
numbers (in a measure-theoretic sense, the rationals have *measure
zero*). Most of analysis lives in the irrational realm.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** Stretch S2 explains why crypto avoids irrationals
in practice — and uses *modular* analogues like quadratic residues.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development & physics.** Distance computations
(Pythagorean theorem next lesson) routinely produce irrational
results. Game engines compute $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ countless
times per frame.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Standard paper sizes (A4, A3) are scaled by
$\sqrt{2}$ — so each step preserves aspect ratio. Musical
intervals (the 12-tone equal temperament) are built on
$\sqrt[12]{2}$. Architecture and design routinely use irrational
ratios.
:::
